items.q: In what direction does a magnetic field exert a force on a current-carrying wire?
items.correct: Perpendicular to both the current direction and the magnetic field direction (Fleming's left hand rule).
items.misconception: The force is in the same direction as the magnetic field.
items.random_wrong: The force is in the same direction as the current in the wire.
items.why: Students expect forces to act 'along' visible directions; the perpendicular result is counterintuitive without a vector cross product mental model.

items.q: If the distance between two masses doubles, what happens to the gravitational force between them?
items.correct: It quarters — gravitational force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
items.misconception: It halves — gravitational force is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It doubles — more distance means more space for gravity to act through.
items.why: Students default to linear relationships and forget the inverse-square law; they apply 1/r reasoning instead of 1/r^2.

items.q: Why does increasing the intensity of light below the threshold frequency not eject electrons?
items.correct: Each photon's energy is below the work function; many low-energy photons can't combine to eject an electron.
items.misconception: Enough low-frequency photons should eventually eject an electron if you wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Low-frequency light doesn't have enough mass to push electrons out of the metal.
items.why: Students think energy is cumulative (more photons = more energy delivered), missing that each electron interacts with one photon at a time in the photoelectric effect.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5 km. “7 km” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: It's Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
